key_insight: Elastic surface wrinkling in a compressed hyperelastic half-space can be reinterpreted as a topological phase transition with a quantized winding-number change.
engineering_relevance: This provides a new physics-based framework for predicting and designing instability thresholds in soft structures, with potential applications in programmable smart materials and surface morphing systems.
method: Generalized Stroh formalism with Lie-group metric evolution to model finite-strain geometry.
Mapped the surface impedance matrix to an effective 1D Dirac Hamiltonian.
Tracked the mechanical stretch as a tunable Dirac mass controlling the phase transition.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports an analytical mapping and topological characterization, but does not mention experimental measurements or numerical benchmarking.
watch_item: The topological interpretation is compelling, but practical use will depend on how well the model captures material nonlinearity, boundary conditions, and real-world imperfections.
paper_title: Elastic Surface Instability as a Topological Phase Transition
